response:
It’s a witty, surprisingly moving novel about a jaded middle-aged academic who becomes obsessed with a tiny, almost invisible detail in old artworks. What starts as a niche intellectual curiosity turns into a strange meditation on beauty, mortality, love, and the way people use ideas to avoid their own lives.

What I love most about it is the voice: dry, intelligent, self-mocking, but also vulnerable in ways the narrator barely understands. It manages to be both satirical and sincere. Beneath all the art history and philosophy, it’s really about loneliness, late-life desire, and the possibility that paying close attention to small things can become a way of resisting despair.

It’s one of those books that feels slim and modest, yet somehow keeps opening outward the more you think about it.
